control_description: The entity responds to identified security incidents by executing a defined incident response program to understand, contain, remediate, and communicate security incidents, as appropriate.

evidence_files.description: Beacon Health Technologies incident response policy with HIPAA-specific requirements for PHI breach handling
Q4 2025 incident register with 8 incidents including PHI involvement flags and false positive determinations
Security exception register including false positive documentation for INC-2025-504 and tabletop exercise postponement

noise_files.description: Q4 2025 HIPAA security awareness training completion report — training compliance not directly relevant to incident response control effectiveness
Q4 2025 change management log — change management process distinct from incident response controls

prompt: You are a SOC 2 auditor evaluating Beacon Health Technologies' incident response controls for the Q4 2025 observation period (October 1 - December 31, 2025). Beacon is a HIPAA Business Associate processing protected health information (PHI). Review the incident response policy, incident log, exception register, and any supporting documentation. Assess whether CC7.3 requirements for incident response are met. Be careful to distinguish between genuine control gaps and situations that appear problematic but have valid explanations in the evidence.

findings.title: INC-2025-504 (SEV-3) has no post-mortem despite involving a 4.7 GB data transfer
findings.description: INC-2025-504 involved a 4.7 GB outbound data transfer flagged as anomalous. The incident is marked as 'Not Required' for post-mortem, which might appear concerning given the volume of data involved. However, the incident log explicitly marks this as a false positive — the investigation confirmed the transfer was an authorized DR bucket replication created by DevOps engineer Mike Torvalds. The exception register (SEC-EXC-2025-091) documents the false positive determination with full investigation details: S3 bucket ownership verified via CloudTrail, transfer content matched scheduled replication payload, and the engineering team was reminded of the new-resource notification procedure. Policy Section 5.1 explicitly states false positives do not require post-mortems. This is NOT a finding.
findings.expected_reasoning: The model should notice INC-2025-504 has no post-mortem and involves a large data transfer. It should then check the false_positive column (marked 'Yes'), the exception register (SEC-EXC-2025-091 documenting the false positive determination), and the policy (Section 5.1: false positives do not require post-mortems). The investigation was thorough and properly documented. A competent auditor would dismiss this.

findings.title: INC-2025-503 (SEV-2) post-mortem never completed — still 'Not Started' 8 weeks after resolution
findings.description: INC-2025-503 (unauthorized API calls from external account traced to former contractor) was resolved on November 6, 2025. The post-mortem was due by November 13 (5 business days per policy Section 5.1). As of end of Q4, the post-mortem status is 'Not Started' — not late, not in progress, but never begun. This is a SEV-2 incident involving unauthorized access from a former contractor whose IAM cross-account trust was not revoked. The root cause (stale third-party access) is exactly the type of issue that needs a post-mortem to drive corrective action. Zero action items have been created. No exception was filed for the missed deadline.
findings.expected_reasoning: INC-2025-503 is SEV-2 with post_mortem_status='Not Started' and post_mortem_due=2025-11-13. The current date is well past November 13. Unlike INC-2025-504 (false positive with valid exemption), this is a confirmed security incident — former contractor access was not revoked. No exception filed. The lack of a post-mortem means the root cause (stale cross-account trust) has no corrective action tracking.

findings.title: INC-2025-505 (SEV-1 ransomware with PHI) has only 3 of 7 action items complete 5+ weeks after incident
findings.description: INC-2025-505 was a SEV-1 ransomware incident on November 22 that affected the SFTP gateway containing patient data files. The post-mortem is marked complete, but only 3 of 7 action items are finished as of end of Q4. This is a PHI-involving SEV-1 incident — the most critical category for a HIPAA Business Associate. With 4 action items unresolved 5+ weeks after the incident, the corrective actions to prevent recurrence of a ransomware incident involving patient data are lagging.
findings.expected_reasoning: INC-2025-505: SEV-1, ransomware, PHI involved (3 patient data files encrypted). Post-mortem complete but only 3 of 7 action items done. Policy Section 5.3 requires weekly review and 14-day escalation of overdue items. The SFTP gateway processes inbound patient data — preventive actions should be prioritized given PHI involvement.

findings.title: INC-2025-507 (SEV-2 WAF bypass) has only 2 of 5 action items complete
findings.description: INC-2025-507 involved an HTTP request smuggling attack that bypassed the WAF and reached the application layer. The post-mortem is complete, but only 2 of 5 action items are finished. The incident involved a novel HTTP/2 desync technique — meaning existing WAF rules were ineffective. If the remaining 3 action items include WAF rule updates or application-layer protections, the vulnerability may still be exploitable.
findings.expected_reasoning: INC-2025-507: WAF bypass via HTTP/2 desync. Only 2 of 5 action items complete. An emergency WAF rule was deployed (CHG-2025-E043 in the change log), but the remaining action items likely address deeper fixes — application-layer defenses, HTTP/2 parsing improvements, etc. With the WAF bypassed once, incomplete remediation leaves the application vulnerable to similar attacks.

findings.title: Q4 tabletop exercise postponed — quarterly testing requirement not met
findings.description: The exception register (SEC-EXC-2025-088) shows the Q4 2025 tabletop exercise was postponed to January 2026 due to SOC 2 audit preparation workload. Policy Section 8 requires quarterly tabletop exercises. While the postponement was approved by the CEO and has a valid justification, the Q4 2025 quarterly testing requirement was not met during the observation period. The annual simulated exercise was completed in September 2025, providing some testing coverage, but the quarterly cadence was broken.
findings.expected_reasoning: Policy requires quarterly tabletops (Section 8). Q4 tabletop postponed per SEC-EXC-2025-088. While CEO-approved, the testing gap means the IRP was not validated during a quarter that included 2 SEV-1 incidents (INC-2025-501 and INC-2025-505). The irony: actual incidents occurred during the quarter the tabletop was skipped, but a tabletop exercise tests coordination and process, not just response to real events.
